habitat: Rocky slopes, roadsides, and disturbed ground in dry temperate regions
blurb: The quintessential cottage-garden spire: hollyhocks send up 5–8 ft stalks packed with crepe-paper flowers in every color from white to near-black, a magnet for painted lady butterflies and bumblebees. Biennial or short-lived perennial, they self-sow reliably into a permanent colony wherever the soil is lean and drainage is sharp.
sun: Full sun (6+ hours); tolerates light afternoon shade
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established — avoid wet feet
spread: Self-sows freely; forms loose colonies over time
seasons: Bold basal rosettes emerge in spring; tall spires of saucer flowers in summer (June–August); dried disk-shaped seedheads on bare stalks persist into fall and winter
wildlife: Bees, hummingbirds, and butterflies work the flowers; larval host for painted lady butterfly (Vanessa cardui); songbirds eat the seeds
toxic: Non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses; flowers and young leaves are edible; sap may cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals

care.hardiness: Hardy in USDA zones 3–9 — easily perennial across the entire Front Range (Boulder is zone 5b–6a). In Boulder's climate hollyhocks behave as biennials: seed germinates and forms a basal rosette the first year, then blooms the second year and dies. Self-seeding means a well-established patch renews itself year after year, so they feel perennial in the garden even though individual plants are short-lived.[1]
care.planting: Sow seed directly outdoors in the garden in late spring to early summer (May–June on the Front Range), or wait until late summer (August) to sow for plants that overwinter as rosettes and bloom the following summer — the late-summer sow is the classic biennial timing. Seed can also be started indoors 6–8 weeks before the last frost (around mid-March in Boulder) for same-year bloom on longer-season plants, though outdoor direct sowing is simpler and hollyhocks resent root disturbance. Transplant nursery starts or move seedlings in spring (April–May) once the ground is workable, handling roots carefully. In a container, hollyhocks grow fine through summer but their tall, top-heavy spires demand a sizeable, weighted pot (≥15 gallon) staked for wind; container roots are far less cold-hardy than in-ground plants, so overwinter the pot in an unheated garage or sink it in the soil if you want the rosette to survive for a second-year bloom.[1]
care.sun: Full sun is essential — at least 6 hours of direct sun daily. In full sun the spire stays upright and bloom is heaviest; in afternoon shade on the dry Front Range the stems get taller and floppier and bloom declines. Light morning sun with afternoon shade is barely tolerable but not ideal. The plant handles the intense Colorado sun well.[1]
care.soil: Tolerates a wide range of soils including clay, loam, and sand, and grows in acid, neutral, or alkaline conditions — so Boulder's alkaline clay-loam is not an obstacle. What it cannot stand is consistently wet, waterlogged soil, which rots the crown and roots. Improve heavy clay drainage with coarse organic matter, or site the plant on a slight slope or at the base of a wall (the traditional cottage placement) where drainage is sharp. Rich, well-fertilized soil produces lush leafy growth but fewer flowers and worse rust; lean to moderate fertility suits it.[1]
care.water: Low to moderate. Once established, hollyhocks are drought-tolerant and prefer to dry out between waterings — they thrive in the same hot, dry summers that define the Front Range. Water new transplants and seedlings regularly through the first season; established plants need a deep soak every 1–2 weeks during the hottest stretches, but less is fine if rain falls. Avoid overhead watering, which wets the foliage and speeds the spread of rust and leaf-spot fungal diseases — water at the base. In heavy clay that drains poorly, hollyhocks suffer from crown rot in wet winters or springs.[1]
care.spacing: Space plants 18–36 in. apart depending on your goal: 18 in. for a tight cottage-garden backdrop, 24–36 in. for better airflow (which slows rust). Erect rigid stems rarely need staking unless the site is unusually windy.[1]
care.propagation: Seed is the primary and most reliable method: hollyhocks germinate readily without cold stratification (seeds carry no significant dormancy requirement). Sow outdoors in late spring through late summer, pressing seed shallowly into the soil surface and covering lightly (about ¼ in.) — they need light to germinate. Seeds germinate in 10–14 days at 60–70°F. In warm soil they sprout in 1–2 weeks; cool spring soil slows them but doesn't prevent germination. Allow plants to set seed and the colony self-sows reliably year after year — the main way hollyhocks naturalize in cottage gardens. For specific colors, save seed from the plants you want and sow fresh. Division is rarely practical (deep tap root) but established basal offshoots or small rosettes can be severed and transplanted in early spring.[1]
care.sow: Sow outdoors from late spring through late summer (May–August on the Front Range): press seeds into the soil surface and cover about ¼ in., keep moist until germination in 10–14 days. For blooms the same season, start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost (~mid-March in Boulder) — but direct outdoor sowing is easier and hollyhocks hate root disturbance. Self-sown seedlings that appear in late summer will overwinter as rosettes and bloom the following summer.[1]
care.bloom: Tall flower spikes open progressively from bottom to top, so bloom extends over 4–6 weeks from late June through August on the Front Range. Individual flowers last only a day or two, but fresh buds open daily up the spike. Removing spent blooms (deadheading lower flowers as the spike matures) may slightly extend the season but the main effect is tidiness. Cut spikes to the ground after bloom to tidy and reduce rust spore production; leave a few spikes to set seed if you want self-sown replacements.[1]
care.feeding: Light to moderate. Apply a balanced or low-nitrogen fertilizer (or a thin layer of compost) in early spring as growth resumes. Avoid high-nitrogen feeding, which produces lush, floppy, disease-prone growth at the expense of flowering. Established, self-seeding colonies on reasonable soil often need no supplemental feeding at all.[1]
care.maintenance: The main annual chore is rust management (see Troubles). Beyond that: stake or shelter in very windy spots, since the tall spires can snap in summer storms. Cut spent flower spikes to the ground after bloom to prevent rust reinfection next year and to control where seeds fall. Leave a few spikes uncut if you want self-sown replacements. In late fall the dead stalk can be cut to the base; the rosette of the first-year plants overwinters at ground level and is cold-hardy.[1]
care.troubles: Hollyhock rust (*Puccinia malvacearum*) is the defining pest of this plant — count on it. Orange-yellow powdery pustules appear on leaf undersides by midsummer, the upper leaf surface shows yellow spots, and heavily infected leaves wither and drop. Rust will not kill the plant outright but it is cosmetically disfiguring and worsens each year if spores are allowed to overwinter on debris. Front Range management: (1) Water at the base, never overhead, to keep foliage dry. (2) Remove and bag (don't compost) infected leaves as soon as you see them. (3) After bloom, cut all stalks to the ground and bag the debris — this removes the principal overwintering spore reservoir and is the single most effective step. (4) Rotate planting spots when practical; avoid planting new seedlings next to badly infected old plants. Fungicides (sulfur-based, neem, or copper) can slow the spread but are not curative; apply preventatively beginning in spring if rust has been severe in past years. Slugs and snails attack young plants and seedlings — use slug bait or diatomaceous earth around seedlings. Japanese beetles and hollyhock weevil can disfigure flowers; hand-pick beetles. Spider mites occur in hot, dry conditions; knock off with a strong water spray.[1]
care.companions: Hollyhocks thrive in the same hot, lean spots as other cottage-garden biennials and short-lived perennials. Classic pairings: climbing roses (both love a hot sunny wall), lavender, and spike speedwell as lower-story contrast to the tall spires; white shasta daisies or phlox for color contrast; prairie coneflower and blanket flower in a more informal planting. Their bold vertical spires read well behind compact mound-formers — coreopsis, catmint, yarrow. Avoid crowding with other rust-susceptible mallows.[1]

edible.card: Flowers as edible garnish or salad; young leaves cooked
edible.summary: The flowers are edible fresh and commonly used as a showy garnish or salad decoration; young leaves can be cooked as a pot-herb. The whole plant is non-toxic to people and pets.[1][2]
edible.parts: The **flowers** (petals and buds) are the best-known edible part: mild-tasting, slightly mucilaginous, used raw as a colorful garnish in salads and cocktails, or floated in beverages. They can also be stuffed or crystallized. Young **leaves** are edible cooked — the mild flavor is palatable as a pot-herb or finely chopped into cooked dishes, though the texture is somewhat rough and mucilaginous. Young **stems** can be eaten raw. The **root** yields a starchy mucilage historically used as a thickener, and a refreshing flower-petal tea is documented in traditional practice.[1][2]
edible.preparation: Flowers need no preparation — rinse and use raw. For leaves, cook briefly as a pot-herb; the mucilaginous texture softens on cooking and the mild flavor blends well with other greens. Choose only young, fresh leaves before the plant bolts; older leaves become tough. The root is a traditional, minor medicinal-culinary item, not a main food crop.[1][2]
edible.caution: The whole plant is listed as non-toxic to dogs, cats, and horses by the ASPCA; no toxic compounds are documented in the literature for this species. However, the sap and leaf hairs can cause mild contact dermatitis (skin irritation) in sensitive individuals — wear gloves when handling if you have sensitive skin. The leaves are mucilaginous and rough-textured; large raw quantities may cause mild digestive discomfort. As with all edible flowers, avoid plants that have been treated with pesticides.[1][3]
